current_fact.fact_text: In 1958, the United States launched Vanguard 1, the first solar-powered satellite and the oldest human-made object still orbiting Earth. Its tiny radio beacons helped scientists refine the shape of our planet and map the upper atmosphere, turning a grapefruit-sized sphere into a quiet, long-term storyteller of near-Earth space.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

current_fact.match_reason: A space-themed, forward-looking satellite milestone pairs naturally with an episode built around 'space' as possibility and energetic, future-facing sounds.

queue.fact_text: In 1950, researchers at the University of California, Berkeley announced the creation of californium, element 98. This intensely radioactive metal is so powerful that milligram quantities can be used to start nuclear reactors and to scan for hidden oil and water deep underground.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

queue.match_reason: A newly forged, highly radioactive element resonates with an 'electric shock' set full of high-voltage guitars and charged energy.

queue.fact_text: In 1966, the deep-sea submersible Alvin located a missing American hydrogen bomb off the coast of Spain, three months after it fell into the Mediterranean. The search turned a small research submarine into a Cold War legend and showed how ocean science could suddenly intersect with nuclear geopolitics.

episode.title: Morning Drops #33 - Immerse
episode.description: An analogy between diving, one of Gaia's great passions, and the act of immersing in life itself. A reflection on what happens when we go below the surface: when we let ourselves be fully present. From moments of calm suspension to warm, rhythmic flow, the selection moves like water — fluid, organic, and alive. An invitation to slow down, listen deeper, and float.
songs: Pino D'Angiò - Un Po' d'Uva e un Liquore

queue.match_reason: A deep-ocean search for a hidden bomb aligns with an episode themed around immersion, diving below the surface, and fluid, underwater-like movement.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 1968, more than 6,000 sheep mysteriously died in Utah's Skull Valley after U.S. Army nerve gas tests went awry. The incident, often called the Dugway sheep kill, became a chilling symbol of how invisible chemical weapons can ripple far beyond any intended battlefield.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

queue.match_reason: An invisible, controversial technology with massive side effects mirrors an episode about Auto-Tune: another hidden tool whose subtle use profoundly changed its field.

queue.fact_text: In 1862, Finland opened its first railway line, the Päärata, linking Helsinki to Hämeenlinna. The iron track stitched together distant towns across forests and lakes, accelerating the country's shift from scattered settlements to a more connected, modern society.

episode.title: Sedna FM #05 - On The Go - Zero Zero
episode.description: An energetic mix for when you're on the move. Zero Zero captures that feeling of starting fresh, moving forward, and embracing the journey. Perfect for travel, walks, or just getting things done.
songs: Grace Jones - Pull up to the bumper
Easy star all star - Soul love
James Brown - Talking loud and saying nothing (remix)

queue.match_reason: A new railway line and the thrill of going somewhere resonate with a travel-ready, kinetic mix made for motion and fresh starts.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 1963, Bali’s sacred volcano Mount Agung erupted catastrophically, sending ash 10 kilometers into the sky and killing more than 1,100 people. The eruption not only reshaped the island’s landscape but also its rituals, as many Balinese interpreted it as a spiritual warning from the gods.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: Awe-inspiring, dangerous natural forces — a towering volcano and Arctic megafauna — both evoke nature’s vast power and beauty, mirrored by cinematic, atmospheric music.

queue.fact_text: In 1988, Avianca Flight 410, a Colombian Boeing 727, crashed into a mountainside shortly after takeoff from Cúcuta, killing all 143 people aboard. The tragedy exposed how even a routine departure can hinge on razor-thin margins of navigation, training, and terrain awareness.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

queue.match_reason: A deadly crash in mountainous terrain is paired with a reflective, weather-themed episode about storms, transitions, and finding hope after dark moments.

queue.fact_text: In 1957, a Douglas C-47 aircraft crashed into Mount Manunggal in Cebu, killing Philippine President Ramon Magsaysay and 24 others. The sudden loss of a popular leader deep in the mountains turned a remote crash site into a place of national mourning and pilgrimage.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

queue.match_reason: The death of a head of state in a sudden accident sits alongside an introspective episode about abrupt life changes and the long arc of healing.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 1988, Eritrean fighters launched a coordinated attack on the Nadew Command, a key Ethiopian army corps in Eritrea, in the opening of the Battle of Afabet. Within days, one of Ethiopia’s most powerful military units was shattered, reshaping the trajectory of the Eritrean War of Independence.

episode.title: Morning Drops #24 - The Rise Of Afrobeat
episode.description: An exploration of the rise of Afrobeat, tracing its roots and evolution as a genre that blends African rhythms with jazz, funk, and highlife. A celebration of the infectious energy and cultural significance of this influential musical movement.

queue.match_reason: A decisive African independence battle is matched with an episode celebrating Afrobeat and African musical freedom, both speaking to liberation and resistance.

queue.fact_text: In 1960, U.S. President Dwight D. Eisenhower signed a secret National Security Council directive authorizing covert efforts to undermine Cuba’s new government, setting the path toward the Bay of Pigs invasion. The document turned geopolitical anxiety into a clandestine playbook that would echo through decades of U.S.–Latin American relations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

queue.match_reason: Covert intervention and political manipulation fit with a fiercely political rock episode about confrontation, resistance, and questioning power.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 1948, Belgium, France, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, and the United Kingdom signed the Treaty of Brussels, a mutual defense pact that paved the way for NATO. In a Europe still traumatised by war, it was an early experiment in turning former rivals into a security community.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A cross-border alliance among European neighbors is reflected in a musical voyage that sails across multiple European and Mediterranean cultures, emphasizing shared spaces.

queue.fact_text: In 2004, a wave of unrest swept Kosovo, leaving more than 22 people dead and around 200 wounded, while dozens of Serbian Orthodox churches and two mosques were destroyed. The violence exposed just how fragile coexistence remained in the region, even years after the formal end of war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: Ethnic and religious violence is counterbalanced with an episode about travel, perspective shifts, and gentler cross-cultural connections, quietly suggesting different ways of meeting 'the other'.

queue.fact_text: In 2000, more than 530 members of the Ugandan cult Movement for the Restoration of the Ten Commandments of God died in a church fire, with hundreds more bodies later found at other sites. What first looked like a mass suicide increasingly appeared to be a carefully orchestrated mass murder by the group’s leadership.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: The manipulation and betrayal inside a doomsday cult parallels an episode about a sharp, critical lyricist who often confronts exploitation, dogma, and abuse of power.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 1979, Scotland’s Penmanshiel Tunnel collapsed during engineering works, killing two workers and burying part of the main East Coast rail line. Rather than excavate the unstable rock, engineers simply built a new stretch of track around the hill, leaving the tunnel sealed like a fossil in the landscape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #28 - Under Scottish Skies
episode.description: Gaia shares a personal moment of transition — a shift in seasons, in rhythm, and in life. From this space of quiet change comes a musical journey through Scotland: not the usual names, but voices that speak from the edges. A personal ritual and a small act of curiosity: an invitation to notice what grounds us when everything moves.
songs: Paolo Nutini - Pencil Full of Lead

queue.match_reason: A tragic tunnel collapse in Scotland pairs with an episode explicitly rooted in Scottish landscapes and the quiet shifts that shape a place and its people.

queue.fact_text: Also in 1979, Aeroflot Flight 1691 crashed while attempting an emergency landing at Moscow’s Vnukovo International Airport, killing 58 people. A false fire alarm led to rushed decisions in a crowded sky, showing how small technical glitches can cascade into disaster.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

queue.match_reason: A crash triggered by a misleading alarm echoes an episode about our complex dependence on technology and how systems designed to help us can create new risks and anxieties.

queue.fact_text: In 1992, white South Africans voted in a referendum to end apartheid, with nearly 69% supporting reforms that would dismantle the system of racial segregation. It was an extraordinary moment in which a privileged electorate formally chose to vote away its own legal supremacy.

episode.title: Morning Drops #08 - (un)Boxing
episode.description: An episode about breaking free from expectations and labels. Music that helps us unbox ourselves and embrace who we truly are.
songs: Kendrick Lamar - The Blacker the Berry

queue.match_reason: The vote to end apartheid is a collective act of breaking out of racist boxes, aligned with an episode explicitly about dismantling labels and confronting identity and injustice.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 180, the Roman emperor Marcus Aurelius died, leaving his eighteen-year-old son Commodus as sole ruler of the Roman Empire. His accession marked a shift from a philosopher-king to a notoriously erratic leader who blurred politics with personal spectacle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #10 - The One With Many Names
episode.description: Yasmin guides us through the glamorous world of Ghost, the Swedish band that turned rock into ritual. From masked clergy to shifting identities, diving into the music and myth of one of the most theatrical acts of our time.

queue.match_reason: A young emperor who turns rule into theatre fits with an episode about a band built on ritual, masks, and performance, both mixing power with spectacle and role-play.

queue.fact_text: Back in 45 BC, Julius Caesar won his final victory at the Battle of Munda, defeating the Pompeian forces of Titus Labienus and Pompey the Younger in Spain. It was a brutal, close-run fight that effectively ended the Roman Republic’s last major resistance to Caesar’s dictatorship.

episode.title: Sedna FM #01 - November Bomb
episode.description: The first Family & Friends radio episode from Yasmin Sarbaoui and Gaia Parolini. Where it all began — a life soundtrack that launched Sedna FM into the universe. Raw, heartfelt, and full of the energy that defines everything that followed.
songs: Canserbero - De la vida como pelicula, tragedia, comedia y ficcion

queue.match_reason: Caesar’s last, decisive battle that defines everything after mirrors Sedna FM’s explosive first episode that sets the tone for the station’s own history.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 1805, the French-backed Italian Republic transformed into the Kingdom of Italy, with Napoleon Bonaparte crowning himself its king. It was both a political rebranding and a theatrical statement, as a French emperor draped himself in Italian royal symbolism.

episode.title: Morning Drops #22 - Sicilians On The Dolomites
episode.description: Hosted by Yasmin, a musical journey inspired by the mountains. Joined by two special guests, exploring the tracks they've chosen, hearing the stories behind their picks, and discovering the personal memories each song brings to life — a blend of music, voices, and landscapes you can hear.
songs: Florian Pellissier Quintet - Coup de foudre à Thessalonique
Lucio Dalla & Francesco De Gregori - Ma come fanno i marinai

queue.match_reason: A French-shaped Kingdom of Italy is nicely echoed by an episode where Italian and a touch of French music meet in the mountains, blending identities and landscapes.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 1776, British troops evacuated Boston after George Washington’s forces placed captured artillery on the heights around the city. The Siege of Boston ended not with a final clash, but with a quiet withdrawal that gave the young American Revolution one of its first major victories.

episode.title: Morning Drops #01 - Timeless Legacy
episode.description: Gaia guides us through timeless music to start the morning with the right foot! The first episode of the Morning Drops series — classic sounds that never fade.
songs: Roberta Flack - Killing me softly with his song

queue.match_reason: A foundational victory in a revolution that birthed a new nation pairs with a first 'legacy' episode, both concerned with beginnings that echo far into the future.

queue.fact_text: In 1988, the same year as Flight 410, the Eritrean People’s Liberation Front’s offensive at Afabet cracked the Nadew Command, one of Ethiopia’s strongest formations. The battle became a turning point, showing how mobility and local knowledge could defeat a larger, conventionally organised force.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: A desert-grounded guerrilla victory in East Africa aligns with an episode about desert-born music, exile, and resistance, both telling stories shaped by arid land and struggle.

queue.fact_text: In 2000, in Uganda’s Kanungu district, a cult’s promise of apocalyptic salvation ended in fire and mass death, but investigators later discovered multiple graves, suggesting a long-running pattern of killings. The movement’s obsession with the Ten Commandments twisted moral language into a tool of control.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

queue.match_reason: The catastrophic result of blind, authoritarian belief contrasts with an episode about curiosity, critical learning, and embracing doubt, offering a different path than unquestioned doctrine.

queue.fact_text: On March 17, 1988, Avianca Flight 410’s crash near the Venezuelan border highlighted the challenges of flying over rugged Andean terrain, where clouds can hide mountains until it is too late. The accident spurred changes in training and procedures that quietly make modern flights safer.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

queue.match_reason: A tragic flight over dramatic terrain contrasts with a gentle, communal journey by sea; both are about navigation and how we move together through potentially dangerous spaces.

queue.fact_text: In 2004’s Kosovo unrest, not only people but also places of worship became targets: 35 Serbian Orthodox sites and two mosques were destroyed or damaged. The attacks showed how buildings can hold memories so powerful that, in a conflict, they’re treated almost like enemies themselves.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Violence against sacred architecture is contrasted with an episode about a deep, emotionally charged cultural tradition; both show how art and religion carry identity in contested regions.

published.fact_text: In 1958, the United States launched Vanguard 1, the first solar-powered satellite and the oldest human-made object still orbiting Earth. Its tiny radio beacons helped scientists refine the shape of our planet and map the upper atmosphere, turning a grapefruit-sized sphere into a quiet, long-term storyteller of near-Earth space.

episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once
